food_subgroup: Thai set meal with fish and fruit-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Thai mango seabass specifically; FOODON_03315091 (fish dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (sea bass, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific Thai mango seabass set code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai mango seabass set meal.

serving.description: 1 set for 2 persons (approx. 2 plates of rice + seabass + mango salad)
serving.natural_language_portion: One set for two persons comprising approximately 800 g total, including two portions of steamed jasmine rice (approx. 300 g combined), one whole or half seabass with mango dressing (approx. 400 g), and a shared green mango salad (approx. 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Seabass cooked (FDC ID 175159), Mango raw (FDC ID 169910), Cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), and FAO/INFOODS SEA fish sauce reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai mango seabass set meal exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked seabass (FDC 175159), raw mango (FDC 169910), cooked white rice (FDC 168878), and fish sauce (FDC 173610). Approximate component weight proportions per 100 g of total set: seabass 35%, jasmine rice 40%, green mango 15%, sauce/condiments 10%. Values represent a per-100 g average of the assembled set.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice GI reference and mixed meal estimation
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai mango seabass set. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed jasmine white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database, food ID 2905) blended with low-GI components: green mango (GI ~41, analogous to raw mango per Atkinson et al. 2021) and fish/protein components (GI ~0). Weighted composite GI calculated using carbohydrate contribution method: rice contributes ~68% of total carbohydrate, mango ~22%, sauce ~10%. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 134.4 g carbohydrate in 800 g serving) / 100 ≈ 75.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: This set meal contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g, yielding an estimated 2,480 mg sodium per full 800 g set; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion to half the set to keep sodium intake within the 1,500–2,000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by jasmine white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The set has a medium GI of approximately 56 and a high glycemic load per full serving (~75); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce rice portion to one serving and pair with the seabass and mango salad to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Seabass provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g), making this set a heart-friendly choice when sodium from fish sauce is moderated; the green mango component contributes vitamin C and dietary fibre supporting vascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Seabass Set for Two (Thai: เซ็ตปลากะพงมะม่วง) is a Thai restaurant set meal designed for two diners, featuring a whole or half Asian seabass (barramundi, pla kapong) prepared with a green mango dressing or salad, accompanied by steamed jasmine rice. The dish originates from Thai cuisine and is commonly served in Thai restaurants in Singapore, such as Sanook Kitchen. The seabass is typically steamed or lightly fried and dressed with a tangy-sweet sauce made from green mango (青芒果), fish sauce, lime juice, palm sugar, chilli, and garlic, creating a balance of sour, sweet, salty, and spicy flavours characteristic of Thai cooking. The green mango provides a crisp, tart contrast to the tender, mild white flesh of the seabass. Nutritionally, the set is a moderate-energy meal (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a good protein contribution from the fish (~8.2 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from jasmine rice (~16.8 g per 100 g). Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce (~310 mg per 100 g), which is a clinical consideration for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), driven primarily by jasmine white rice. The meal is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for those with fish allergies. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control of the rice component is advised. The set is a culturally significant Thai sharing meal format, popular in Singapore's Thai restaurant scene.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this specific Thai mango seabass set meal was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients (cooked seabass, raw mango, cooked white rice, fish sauce). GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (jasmine white rice) blended with lower-GI components. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-specific preparation variability.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, sauce quantity, and rice portion size. 2. The food_name has been canonicalised from the field survey menu entry '1076D $43.80 Mango Seabass Set for 2 青芒果' to 'Mango Seabass Set for Two'; the price and menu code are not part of the canonical food identity. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimation. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as seabass and plant-based ingredients in this preparation do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 7. GI estimated at 56 using carbohydrate contribution weighting from jasmine white rice (GI 72) and green mango (GI 41 analogous); this is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement if available. 8. Sodium is a primary clinical concern; fish sauce contributes the majority of sodium in this dish. 9. The Chinese name 青芒果 (green mango) appears in the original field survey listing and is preserved as an alias; the full Chinese set name is 芒果鲈鱼套餐（两人份）. 10. This is a Thai cuisine dish; mandatory Thai-script local name is provided. Chinese names are provided as the dish is served in Singapore and the original listing includes Chinese characters. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish.
